title: A realistic toy experiment
learningObjectives: Explain a realistic toy experiment in plain language
Write down the objective and identify its assumptions
Design one test that could falsify an over-strong interpretation

whyThisLesson.text: Before training on language-model activations, use a toy world where the answer is known. The toy needs a latent concept, several partial views, nuisance variation, and deliberate intruders. Then you can tell whether set pooling recovers the intended direction or merely exploits an easier cue.

intuition.analogy: A toy experiment is a wind tunnel. It is simpler than a real aircraft, but it must still contain the forces that could make the design fail. Here, the known latent direction is the thing we want to recover; view noise and intruders are the ways the experiment can fool us.
intuition.analogyLimit: The toy model tests a mechanism under controlled assumptions. It does not predict a gain on language-model activations until that experiment is run.
intuition.text: A good toy problem has known latent causes, partial views, nuisance variation, and a metric that can distinguish recovery of the intended factor from a shortcut.

toyExample.description: Sample a two-dimensional latent direction for each concept. Produce several noisy views around it, with some views replaced by intruders. Compare the cosine of one view with the cosine of the pooled set estimate against the known latent direction.

steps.result: For example, let the latent concept be a unit vector at 40 degrees in a two-dimensional space.
steps.insight: The ground truth lets you measure recovery directly instead of judging a visually plausible feature label.

steps.result: Add independent noise to each view, then average the views. Compare the pooled vector and a single view with the known latent direction using cosine similarity.
steps.insight: Independent noise averages out; a shared shortcut or an intruder does not.

steps.result: Replace some valid views with samples from a different latent direction and repeat the comparison.
steps.insight: If the pooled estimate collapses under a few intruders, the set encoder needs a robustness mechanism, not a stronger claim.

visual.question: Which assumption behind a realistic toy experiment changes the result most?
visual.description: Move one control at a time. Treat the display as a toy model, then write down the real experiment it suggests.

technical.text: The lab implements the data-generating side of the experiment, not a trained language model. A latent vector c is sampled first. Each clean view is c plus independent noise; an intruder is sampled around a different direction. Mean pooling is the baseline set estimator. Its recovery score is cosine(pool(x_i), c). This isolates one question: when does combining several views improve recovery of a known shared factor?

mathematics.explanation: For this lesson, the expression makes the central trade-off explicit. A good toy problem has known latent causes, partial views, nuisance variation, and a metric that can distinguish recovery of the intended factor from a shortcut.
mathematics.whyNeeded: It says precisely what the method is being rewarded for.
mathematics.ifRemoved: You could not tell whether two implementations were solving the same problem.
mathematics.encourages: A testable prediction about the chosen objective.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: The toy model tests a mechanism under controlled assumptions. It does not predict a gain on language-model activations until that experiment is run.

methodComparison.question: What must be held fixed?
methodComparison.practicalImplication: The ablation identifies which added component caused a gain.

evidence.claim: Multi-view causal representation learning studies when partial views can identify latent variables under explicit assumptions.
evidence.note: The toy borrows the discipline of known latent variables and controlled views; it does not reproduce the paper's full theorem.

failureModes.name: A score that answers the wrong question
failureModes.mechanism: The objective rewards a proxy while the paper describes a stronger scientific property.
failureModes.symptom: The headline metric rises, but seed stability, controls, or interventions disagree.
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Add a matched negative control and one counterfactual intervention.
failureModes.mitigation: Either narrow the claim or add the missing measurement.
failureModes.setconcaRelevance: Report both the intended benefit and the most plausible shortcut for every SetConCA loss term.

setconcaConnection.relevance: A realistic toy experiment determines how SetConCA should represent, group, or evaluate activation views.
setconcaConnection.whichPart: The choice of sparse code, view set, coordination loss, or evaluation.
setconcaConnection.whichDesignChoice: Match the pointwise SAE and SetConCA on model, activation site, width, sparsity budget, and compute.
setconcaConnection.whichFailure: Mistaking stronger agreement for recovery of the intended concept.
setconcaConnection.whichExperiment: Compare fidelity, feature stability, selectivity, and controlled interventions; report each result separately.
setconcaConnection.alternativeExplanation: An apparent gain may come from extra context, effective capacity, or easier positives rather than set coordination.

exercises.prompt: A paper claims that a realistic toy experiment improved interpretability. What one result would you ask for before accepting that claim?
exercises.reveal: Ask for the smallest missing control: a matched baseline, several seeds, a negative control, or an intervention that tests the claimed mechanism.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Explain a realistic toy experiment to a colleague, then name one thing the method cannot establish by itself.

nextLesson.bridge: Next: Hypotheses, baselines, and ablations. The next idea reuses this lesson's representation and makes a new assumption explicit.

fields.label: Falsifiable claim
fields.prompt: Write the smallest claim this lesson supports.

fields.label: Control
fields.prompt: What control would rule out a shortcut?
